md: There are two ways to measure angles. You already know one: **degrees**, where a full circle is $360¬Α$. Here's the other one βÄî **radians** βÄî and why mathematicians prefer it.

The choice of $360$ is *historical*, not mathematical. The Babylonians used base 60, and $360$ has a lot of nice divisors (you can split a circle into halves, thirds, fourths, fifths, sixths, ... cleanly). Convenient βÄî but arbitrary.
Take a circle of radius $r$. Cut out an arc of length $r$ along the edge. The angle that arc subtends βÄî measured at the center βÄî is **$1$ radian**.
On a unit circle (radius $= 1$), the arc length and the angle in radians are the *same number*. The angle equals the arc length. That's the whole idea.

Drag the slider in the widget. The red arc highlights the angle. Its length (shown in the readout below) is exactly the angle in radians. At $\theta = 60¬Α$, the arc is about $1.047$ βÄî that's $\frac{\pi}{3}$ radians.
A full circle has circumference $2\pi r$. On a unit circle, that's $2\pi$ βÄî so a full revolution is $2\pi$ radians. The same revolution is also $360¬Α$. So:
From these, you can convert anything:

blocks.title: Common values worth memorizing
blocks.body: $30¬Α = \tfrac{\pi}{6}$ βÄî $45¬Α = \tfrac{\pi}{4}$ βÄî $60¬Α = \tfrac{\pi}{3}$ βÄî $90¬Α = \tfrac{\pi}{2}$ βÄî $180¬Α = \pi$ βÄî $270¬Α = \tfrac{3\pi}{2}$ βÄî $360¬Α = 2\pi$. They come up so often you'll save real time by memorizing.

md: **1. Arc length is just $r\theta$.** On a circle of radius $r$, the arc from angle $0$ to angle $\theta$ (in radians) has length exactly $r\theta$. Try doing that with degrees βÄî you have to multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$. Messy.

**2. Calculus loves radians.** The derivative of $\sin\theta$ is $\cos\theta$ βÄî but **only if $\theta$ is in radians**. In degrees, you'd carry around $\frac{\pi}{180}$ factors forever. So once you reach calculus, radians become the default.
On a circle of radius $r$, the length $s$ of an arc subtended by a central angle $\theta$ (**in radians**) is:

blocks.title: Critical: the angle MUST be in radians
blocks.body: $s = r\theta$ only works when $\theta$ is in radians. If your angle is in degrees, convert first ($\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{180}$), then plug into the formula.

md: **Example 1.** Find the arc length on a circle of radius $5$ subtended by an angle of $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$.
**Example 2.** Find the arc length on a circle of radius $10$ subtended by $60¬Α$.
**Example 3.** A bike wheel of radius $30$ cm rolls without slipping. After it turns through $4$ radians, how far has the bike moved forward?
The distance traveled equals the arc length on the wheel:
### A useful identity hidden in $s = r\theta$
This says: a radian is just *arc length per radius*. The radians and the radius cancel βÄî the result is dimensionless. (That's another reason radians are the 'natural' angle unit.)

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Radians** measure angles by the arc length they cut off on the unit circle. Full circle $= 2\pi$ rad $= 360¬Α$. Convert with $\tfrac{\pi}{180}$ (deg βÜ£ rad) or $\tfrac{180}{\pi}$ (rad βÜ£ deg). **Arc length on a circle of radius $r$:** $s = r\theta$ (with $\theta$ in radians).
